author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: Hey Rob! To solve the problem of the amount of normal force that can be supplied by the springs, could you try using a pair of fine thread bolts (or threaded rod) with locknuts; on each side of the axle so that you could manually tighten them down to the right torque while keeping the force on each side on center. This would make it something in-between a set-screw and a mechanical press.

author: @gidi1899
text:
Hi, I feel your process (printing machine design) has taken you close enough to start messing with Math, so will you make a machine that allows A,B,D,C,D.


A.  Suppose you can use your machine to:
(((1+2))). Draw (start, continue, stop) a straight line from any point to any point continuesly (within some plane of movement).
(((3))). Draw very short lines (points/x's)
Given a straight line between two points, 
 Draw enough straight lines all starting from the same point,
  which is one of the given line points,
  and all have the same length of the given line,
  enough so notice a circle.

B. Suppose you can build a machien to test over muliples uses the accuracy in producing:
1. straightness
2. point size
3. point to point distance
4. minimal consistently produced angle between lines with a shared point

(assuming you use the machine to draw circles around to points of a line to get 
a drawing of 4 right angles, or does this assumption say something about the machine ?!)

C. Suppose the test machine reports to "your" satisfactory: 
(((4))). Right Angle production is accurare engouh.

D. Then, What is needed from your machine such that:
======
[The parallel postulate]: That, 
if a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on one of it's sides   
    (A) less than two right angles,
then, the two straight lines,
          if produced indefinitely,   (maybe add the functionality of shifting the image)
 meet on side A.
=======

Buttom line = Suppose we did Math using your machine, 
      Can we say something on how machine accuricy relates to
      Mathematical calculation accuricy, like how toruqe tollerence 
      might allow only certain calculating speeds without loosing line straightness,

in other words:

Buttom line = The list of participanted dimentions and materials and balance enquations of the machine in REAL life, VS A List of Mathematical Abilities.

author: @dekutree64
text: Looking forward to seeing the repeatability! My gut feeling is that you'll need closed-loop control to make it work well. Friction drive is great for true zero backlash, but the linear distance per degree of rotation is not guaranteed. It can vary throughout the revolution if the wheel axis isn't precisely on center, and over time as the wheels wear down, and throughout a print as the machine warms up and thermal expansion changes your wheel size.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Robert, great video—nicely organized. Just to add to the conversation, I once came across a fascinating variable transmission concept powering a small, three-wheeled cart. It used a friction-drive mechanism involving a spinning cone connected to a motor’s driveshaft, and a movable disk that slid along the cone’s rubber-coated surface. The spinning disk’s axle connected directly to a single cart wheel, enabling focused power transfer  The position of the disk was manually controlled and it determined performance: when near the narrow end of the cone, the system favored speed; when closer to the wider end of the cone, it emphasized torque. The disk maintained constant contact through spring tension, ensuring consistent engagement as it slid up and down. This clever setup allowed the cart to accelerate smoothly from a standstill, adapting torque and speed with simple manual adjustments.   A 3D-printed cone with a rubber coating could easily bring your concepts to life—combining many of your ideas in a single, functional example.  What do you think?  Call it the Cone-Friction-Torque Drive

author: @chrisgill5692
text: I stopped at 5:25.... one thing with friction is it's related to psi pressure on the contact area and the material coefficient of friction of course .  The smaller the contact area, the higher the psi, so less force required to maintain the friction drive - that reduced required force helps reduce size of supporting mechanisms.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know mate - but you might be missing something here - If the force is constant and you reduce the area it is applied then the pressure will increase - a bit like stiletto heels - but If you reduce the force then the pressure applied to a constant area will also reduce - or is that not true? If it is true than you have made a bit of a circular argument - or at least that's how it seems to me.

replies.author: @chrisgill5692
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering have a read up on "Tribology" and specifically the affects of small contact areas on friction imparted and drag - I think will be super relevant for the efficiency of your friction drives!!

author: @st0rmerc00l8
text: I've thought about friction based mechanisms before and I wonder if using centrifugal force to increase the normal force -> thus increase friction/grip available -> and thus preventing slipping would be meaningful for CVTs or something that is negligible.

replies.author: @ravenmad9225
replies.text: That sounds like a centrifugal clutch.

replies.author: @st0rmerc00l8
replies.text:
@ravenmad9225 not exactly... while the principle is the same, this would be for the purpose of CVTs in cars, which require lots of friction to avoid slipping, and the traditional design of using a belt CVT loses grip at high rpm due to centrifugal forces pulling the belt away from the drum - this is not a problem because it is solved by tensioners, but centrifugal forces could be a benefit to a CVT's design at high RPM if it instead passively enhances grip. (and especially at high rpms when a Turbo kicks in.)

(p.s I have edited my original comment to reflect that it was meant to be about CVTs :D )

author: @nicholasgoodyear7552
text: Hi Robert. I have been following your gearbox series with great interest. i build my own telescope mounts and my latest has a 3 stage friction drive using  disks and shafts. It has a final reduction of over 4000 to 1. It is driven by a nema 14 steppers and controlled by a stm 32 board. With the software i use (PHD 2) it has a tracking accuracy down to .5 seconds of arch for hours on end. My gearbox disks have flat faces bearing on plain straight shafts after seeing this latest video i may have to try this grove idea out. thank you for all the inspiration over the last months.

author: @jimmyjohnson7241
text:
It is always a pleasure to see your latest mechanical designs and concepts. In acknowledgement  of your last video I will make a couple of comments primarily to  stimulate comments from the viewers , since I am certain that you already are aware of the issue:
Friction drives can indeed eliminate backlash, one of the enemies of a precision drive.  What they have a difficulty with is creep, or cumulative error in traversing a path and returning to the original starting point given a fixed input.  With the prototype system you have as a start the  largest error source is likely to be from the inexact effective diameters of two conical surfaces rolling on each other.  Differential pressure from the drive torque  when traversing a closed path containing  two different start/stop sequences is very likely to result in a cumulative timing creep.   The issue although reduced with flat faced rolling contact surfaces remains.  When drive surface wear and temperature variations are included the problem becomes potentially  terminal.
One simple solution that comes readily to mind is the inclusion of a periodic corrector mechanism  to correct for the accumulated error.  Either a home position like solution, or a periodic tooth or hole line up between the friction surfaces.    

The  standard alternative, which is "motion based upon measured position" rather than "drive input"  is near ideal in its ability to remove this problem.  It is unfortunately far from simple or cheap to implement using current methods.  It is the system used in virtually all NC machining.  This solution will also correct for  gear backlash , drive train stress variation, and many other error sources.   With the very low cost of computer logic these predictor-corrector systems are currently the preferred choice.   

I strongly suspect that the solution to these  high cost complex predictor-corrector systems  being implemented in a high precision mechanical system is likely in developing a simple easily implemented position determining corrector component rather than a perfect  input-output mechanical system.

Keep up the excellent work!

author: @partypete2542
text: We had a challenge in college, it was to make a model car with some restrictions, two being: no gears or belts allowed and DC motor can't be attached directly to the wheels. I used a friction a drive with some wood and elastic bands glued around it and the judges were very not happy because I made a clear case that it was indeed not gears, nor was it a belt/chain nor did I connect the DC motor directly to the wheels. Everyone else had used magnets.

replies.author: @st0rmerc00l8
replies.text: Out of the box solutions like that are brilliant! It reminds me of a bridge building challenge I did at school where the teacher gave some restrictions on the materials allowed (wood sticks, pva, carboard, hot glue) and mentioned how the past winning teams used PVA glue instead of hot glue. My team used lots of hot glue, but what we did is we used cardboard triangles at the corners and unlike other teams put them on both the inside and outside to double up each joint. Our bridge survived so much weight that the teacher couldn't stack anymore on!

replies.author: @NadavElmakias
replies.text: @partypete2542 i am wondering if a separate propeller connected to the dc motor with passive wheels would have worked

replies.author: @partypete2542
replies.text: @NadavElmakias Only issue with that solution is that there was a H x W x L limit which was quite limiting, so I don't think a big enough diameter propeller would have been viable but I never did the math for it so it could definitely be an option. Most people just opted for a magnet gearing with high strength magnets and a thin sheet of low friction polymer in between.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is the kind of out of the box thinking i really admire mate - awesome solution!
